mnemonics.title: Template Syntax Memory
mnemonics.content: Remember: 'T' for Template = Think Type - Templates Think about Types before compiling!

mnemonics.title: typename vs class
mnemonics.content: T = Type (think T for Type), C = Class (think C for Class) - both work but T is more accurate for primitives too!

mnemonics.title: SFINAE Memory
mnemonics.content: SFINAE = Substitution Failure Is Not An Error - When template fails, compiler says 'Let me try another way!' instead of giving up.

acronyms.expansion: Substitution Failure Is Not An Error
acronyms.usage: Compile-time technique to enable/disable template functions based on type properties using std::enable_if or decltype
Name lookup mechanism that also searches namespaces of template argument types, used by std::swap for custom types
Compile-time polymorphism technique where derived class inherits from template base class passing itself as parameter

visualTips.visualization: Imagine the compiler as a detective: when you call print(42), it deduces T=int by examining the evidence (the argument type). It automatically figures out the mystery type!
Picture a generic pizza recipe (template) for all toppings, but a SPECIAL recipe (specialization) just for pepperoni that overrides the general one when pepperoni is ordered!
Unlike runtime polymorphism (virtual functions), templates achieve polymorphism at COMPILE-TIME - like having multiple versions pre-made and ready before the program even runs!

analogies.analogy: Think of a function template like a cookie cutter - one design (template) can make cookies (instantiations) of different flavors (types)!
Template parameter T is like a blank spot in a form - it gets filled with whatever specific type you use, like a variable placeholder!
It's like having a general rule for everyone (generic template), but a special exception rule for specific cases (specialization)!
Generic programming is like writing a recipe using 'some vegetable' instead of 'carrot' - the recipe works with ANY vegetable you choose!
